serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of baking soda is approximately 1 teaspoon (4.6 g), used as a leavening agent in baked goods recipes.

nutrient_source.title: Leavening agents, baking soda — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 171377
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Leavening agents, baking soda' (FDC ID 171377) used as primary nutrient reference. This is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Singapore HPB database sodium value of 27,360 mg/100g is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and is retained. All core macros are confirmed zero by chemical formulation — baking soda is pure NaHCO3 with no protein, fat, or carbohydrate content.

gi_source.title: N/A — Baking soda contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate) contains zero carbohydrates by chemical formulation (pure NaHCO3). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates — consistent with the Veritas critical rule for zero/negligible-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list baking soda because GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load; no GI value exists or is needed. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is zero.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (27,360 mg/100g) — the highest of any common food ingredient
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension must strictly limit baking soda use; even a single teaspoon (4.6 g) contributes approximately 1,259 mg of sodium, exceeding half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg, and baking soda should never be consumed as a supplement or antacid without medical supervision.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates, zero sugar, and zero calories — no direct glycaemic impact from baking soda itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baking soda in culinary quantities has no direct glycaemic impact for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, suitability must be assessed based on the total carbohydrate content of the final baked product in which it is used.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content contributes to fluid retention, elevated blood pressure, and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise baking soda use in cooking, as even small culinary quantities (1–5 g per recipe) can contribute 270–1,370 mg of sodium per recipe batch, and should never use baking soda as a home remedy for heartburn or indigestion without cardiology or medical clearance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No anti-inflammatory nutrients present; high sodium may worsen tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Baking soda provides no anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis and its high sodium content may exacerbate tissue swelling; use only in minimal culinary quantities and assess the final baked product for overall RA dietary suitability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baking soda, chemically known as sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), is a fine white crystalline powder used universally as a chemical leavening agent in baking and cooking. It is a processed inorganic compound with an alkaline taste and dry texture. When combined with an acidic ingredient (such as buttermilk, vinegar, lemon juice, or cream of tartar) and moisture, it undergoes a rapid acid-base reaction that releases carbon dioxide gas, causing batters and doughs to rise. It is used in pancakes, cakes, cookies, quick breads, and many savoury recipes. Nutritionally, baking soda is unique: it contains zero calories, zero protein, zero fat, zero carbohydrates, and zero sugar — it is a pure mineral salt. Its sole nutritional characteristic of clinical significance is its extraordinarily high sodium content of 27,360 mg per 100g, making it the highest-sodium ingredient in common culinary use. A single teaspoon (approximately 4.6 g) delivers roughly 1,259 mg of sodium. This makes baking soda a critical concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or any sodium-restricted diet. It has no glycaemic impact and is safe for people with Type 2 Diabetes when used in normal culinary quantities, though the final baked product must be assessed independently. Baking soda should never be consumed in large quantities as a home antacid or supplement without medical supervision due to the risk of metabolic alkalosis and sodium overload.
common_user_queries: Is baking soda the same as bicarbonate of soda?
How much sodium is in baking soda?
Is baking soda safe for high blood pressure?
What is the difference between baking soda and baking powder?
How many calories in baking soda?
Can I use baking soda as an antacid?

used in savoury marinades to tenderise meat

confidence_reason: Baking soda is a pure chemical compound (NaHCO3) with a well-established and universally consistent nutritional profile. Nutrient values are confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171377), an approved primary source, and are consistent with the Singapore HPB database input values. All macros are confirmed zero by chemical formulation. Sodium content of 27,360 mg/100g is consistent across all approved sources.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — baking soda is pure sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), an inorganic mineral salt containing no amino acids or protein. fat_g is naturally zero — baking soda contains no lipids or fatty acids by chemical formulation. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — baking soda contains no carbohydrates, sugars, or starch by chemical formulation. energy_kcal is naturally zero as a direct consequence of zero protein, fat, and carbohydrate. Confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171377) and Singapore HPB database. All vitamin and mineral fields other than sodium are set to 0.0 as baking soda is a single-compound inorganic salt with no organic nutrient content. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; the University of Sydney GI Database does not list baking soda as GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate load. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as the nutrient profile of pure NaHCO3 is fully characterised by its chemical composition. Ontology note: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301428 (sodium bicarbonate), FoodEx2 code A0CKP (sodium bicarbonate/baking soda), and SNOMED CT code 387319002 (sodium bicarbonate substance) are all defensible anchors for this pure chemical compound. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this record as baking soda is a universal processed ingredient with no cuisine-specific origin requiring mandatory local-language naming under the Veritas multilingual rules (G6). Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source updated to cite USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171377 explicitly as an approved source; gi_source updated to reference the University of Sydney GI Database as the authoritative GI authority with a documented explanation of why GI testing is inapplicable for zero-carbohydrate foods; glycemic_index confirmed as null (not numeric zero) per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.
